one_line_takeaway: By hiding malicious logic inside idiomatic code examples and config templates in agent-skill documentation (DDIPE) rather than as explicit instructions, attackers get LLM coding agents to reproduce-and-execute host-level payloads at 11.6-33.5% bypass rates even under production defenses that block explicit injection at 0%, exposing skill supply chains as an unguarded action-space attack surface.
key_finding: No tested configuration is immune: DDIPE achieves bypass rates (BR) of 11.6%-33.5% across all eight configurations, while the explicit natural-language instruction-injection baseline achieves 0% execution under the best-defended setup (Claude Code + Sonnet 4.6). Even that strongest defense still permits 2.3% direct execution (DER). The two defense layers interact asymmetrically: removing architectural guardrails (Claude Code -> OpenHands) amplifies weakly aligned GLM-4.7's DER from 2.4% to 27.1% (11.3x, with 219 of 290 executions being 'sleeper' payloads that Claude Code's permission/path-sandboxing suppressed), but barely moves strongly aligned Sonnet 4.6 (only 5 sleeper activations). A single 479-byte, 9-line pip.conf payload was the only sample executed by all three models under Claude Code, illustrating that minimalist, routine-looking config writes evade semantic-cue alignment detectors. Cross-model disagreement is large (three models agree on only 62.1% of samples; only 1.6% bypass all three), so heterogeneous multi-model verification compresses the joint attack surface from a single model's 13-20% down to 1.6%. Static scanning (SkillScan) catches 90.7% of samples, but 9.3% (99 samples) evade all four detection layers and 27 of those (2.5%) also penetrate model alignment. Responsible disclosure to Claude Code, OpenHands, Codex, and Gemini CLI yielded 4 confirmed vulnerabilities and 2 deployed fixes.
core_contribution: The paper introduces PoisonedSkills, a framework for demonstrating supply-chain poisoning attacks against LLM coding-agent 'skill' ecosystems (SKILL.md packages loaded from open marketplaces). Its central novel technique is Document-Driven Implicit Payload Execution (DDIPE): instead of injecting imperative malicious instructions (which the authors show modern alignment intercepts at high rates), the attacker embeds malicious logic inside benign-looking code examples and configuration templates within skill documentation. Because coding agents treat these examples as authoritative reference implementations and reproduce-then-execute their own generated code, the payload runs on the host without any explicit malicious prompt. The authors also contribute an LLM-driven seed-mutation-validation generation pipeline that scales 81 hand-crafted seeds into 1,070 adversarial skills spanning 15 MITRE ATT&CK-mapped categories, and a two-layer defense decomposition (model-level safety alignment vs. framework-level architectural guardrails) showing the two layers interact asymmetrically. This is explicitly positioned as going beyond prior cited work (ToolTweak, Skill-Inject, PoisonedRAG, indirect prompt injection), which the paper argues stops at tool-selection bias or text-generation pollution and never reaches action-space (host code) execution.
threat_model: Scope is the post-loading phase: the paper assumes the retrieval phase succeeds (the poisoned skill has already entered the agent's context) and studies whether the embedded payload can induce harmful execution despite safety guardrails. The adversary is external with no white-box access to the target agent: they craft an adversarial skill s_adv (metadata m_adv + execution body e_adv) and publish it to a public skill marketplace. The attacker cannot intercept/tamper with victim queries, cannot read the executor's system prompts, and cannot bypass runtime isolation; their only influence path is the skill content itself. This assumption is grounded in current practice (the paper cites the public SkillsMP marketplace hosting 631,813 skills with no mandatory security review, and CVE-2025-59536 as evidence poisoned skill content has reached agent contexts). Attack objectives fall into two classes mapped to enterprise threat matrices: O1 (System Control and Asset Exfiltration, e.g., persistent runtime control, exfiltration of env vars/secrets) and O2 (Infrastructure Poisoning, e.g., backdoored IaC/CI/CD artifacts, tampered pip/npm configs).
limitations: The authors' own stated threats to validity and gaps: (1) Retrieval is assumed to succeed; end-to-end validation of the retrieval/matching phase is left to future work, and production ecosystems with hundreds of skills may dilute attack probability. (2) Same-family overfitting risk: payloads are generated by Claude Opus 4.6, though the authors argue Sonnet 4.6's lower bypass rate (13.5%) vs GLM-4.7 (16.8%) and MiniMax-M2.5 (20.5%) indicates cross-family transfer. (3) The Claude Code vs OpenHands comparison is observational, not a controlled ablation (frameworks differ in multiple dimensions). (4) DDIPE's three camouflage techniques are used together with no per-component ablation. (5) Two of eight configurations (OpenHands+MiniMax 64.5% error, Codex+GPT-5.4 61.0% error) have high framework-specific runtime error rates, so their BR/DER are conservative lower bounds and the main findings rest on the five cells with >87% valid responses. (6) Stealth is measured against only one baseline (explicit instruction injection); stronger baselines like Skill-Inject may narrow the gap. (7) Defense evaluation covers only SkillScan static scanning; dynamic sandboxing and LLM auditing are untested. Coverage excludes the Llama ecosystem and Cursor. Reviewer-noted internal inconsistency: the experimental setup states temperature T=0.0 while the Threats-to-Validity section states T=0.3.

datasets_used: PoisonedSkills: 1,070 author-generated adversarial skills expanded from 81 expert-crafted orthogonal seeds (13.2x expansion), covering 15 MITRE ATT&CK-mapped attack categories
Survey of 150+ real software supply-chain poisoning incidents (2021-2026) drawn from GHSA, NVD, and the OWASP LLM Top 10, used to build the attack taxonomy
Category distribution of the 1,070 skills: supply-chain poisoning 47.5%, env-variable theft 11.9%, credential theft 8.6%, cryptomining 0.4%, IaC attacks 0.1%
quantitative_results: DDIPE bypass rate (BR) range: 11.6%-33.5% across all 8 configurations; explicit-instruction-injection baseline: 0% execution under Claude Code + Sonnet 4.6
Claude Code + Sonnet 4.6: BR 13.5%, DER 2.3% (25 executed, 95% Wilson CI [1.6,3.4])
Claude Code + GLM-4.7: BR 16.8%, DER 2.4% (26 executed)
Claude Code + MiniMax-M2.5: BR 20.5%, DER 13.3% (142 executed)
OpenHands + GLM-4.7: BR 33.5%, DER 27.1% (290 executed); 11.3x amplification vs Claude Code, 219 (75.8%) sleeper payloads
Gemini CLI + Gemini 2.5 Pro: BR 14.0%, DER 4.2%
Strong-alignment control: Sonnet 4.6 produces only 5 sleeper activations across frameworks vs GLM-4.7's 219
Single 479-byte / 9-line pip.conf payload is the only sample executed by all three models under Claude Code
Cross-model agreement: 3 models concur on 62.1% of samples; only 1.6% (17 beyond the pip payload) bypass all three; ensemble compresses attack surface from 13-20% to 1.6%
Static scanning (SkillScan) catches 90.7%; 99 samples (9.3%) evade all four detection layers; 27 (2.5%) also penetrate model alignment
Generation pipeline: 99.1% format validation pass rate; average payload size 1,666 B (Stage 1) -> 4,172 B (Stage 2); executed samples under Claude Code+Sonnet average 1,850 B vs 2,214 B for refused

Per-category DER under Claude Code (Table 4): Config Tamper 6.3% (Sonnet/GLM) vs Supply-Chain Poison 2.8%; MiniMax elevated across all categories (up to 20.3%); reverse shells / container escapes 0% on Sonnet & GLM but 13.7% on MiniMax
LLM-as-a-Judge vs 2 human experts on 10% sample: Cohen's kappa = 0.88
Responsible disclosure: 4 confirmed vulnerabilities, 2 deployed fixes
baselines_compared: Natural-language / explicit instruction injection (imperative forced-action prompts, cited refs Liu et al. and Zhan et al.): 0% execution vs DDIPE 2.3% under Claude Code + Sonnet 4.6; authors note this is a single lower-bound baseline
Benchmark-size comparison (not a run baseline): the 1,070-sample dataset exceeds sample counts reported by ToolTweak and Skill-Inject
Two-layer framework comparison: model-only alignment (OpenHands) vs multi-layer architecture review (Claude Code) vs containerized sandbox (Codex) vs cloud-native safety filtering (Gemini CLI)
